137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

147

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.

213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:

215	```latex

217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

8590	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
8591	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
8592	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
8593	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
8594	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
8595	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
8596	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
8597	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
8598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
8599	
8600	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
8601	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
8602	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
8603	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
8604	
8605	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
8606	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
8607	  sheafification of
8608	  \[
8609	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
8610	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
8611	  \]
8612	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
8613	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
8614	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
8615	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
8616	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
8617	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
8618	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
8619	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
8620	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
8621	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
8622	
8623	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
8624	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
8625	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
8626	  for every \(k\),
8627	  \[
8628	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
8629	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
8630	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
8631	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
8632	  \]
8633	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
8634	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
8635	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
8636	
8637	  \emph{Proof details.} The argument rests on three identifications.
8638	
8639	  \emph{(1) The cohomology-presheaf identification.} The objectwise homology in degree
8640	  \(n\) of the pushed-forward resolution complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\), evaluated at an open
8641	  \(V\), is identified with the absolute cohomology presheaf
8642	  \(V \mapsto H^n(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}) = \operatorname{Ext}^n\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8643	  \mathcal{G}\bigr)\). Concretely: \(\operatorname{Ext}^n(X, Y)\) over an injective resolution of
8644	  the second argument \(Y\) is identified with the \(n\)-th cohomology of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)
8645	  cochain complex \(H^n\bigl(\operatorname{Hom}(X[0], \mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)\), via the composite
8646	  of the Ext-to-cohomology-class equivalence and the cohomology-class-to-homology equivalence of the
8647	  Hom complex (the equivalences of Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homcomplex_mathlib}). Taking \(X = j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)}\) and \(Y =
8648	  \mathcal{G}\), the degree-\(k\) Hom group \(\operatorname{Hom}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}(V)},
8649	  \mathcal{I}^k)\) is identified with \(\Gamma(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{I}^k) = (f_*\mathcal{I}^k)(V)\) by
8650	  the corepresentability \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \dashv \Gamma(U, -)\) of the absolute-cohomology setup,
8651	  degreewise and naturally; the \(\mathbb{N}\)/\(\mathbb{Z}\) indexing of the two cochain complexes is
8652	  matched by the resolution's degreewise comparison isomorphism. The right-derived side is settled by
8653	  the already-used identification of \(R^n f_*\) with the homology of \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\)
8654	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}); no separate ``right-derived equals Ext'' comparison
8655	  lemma is needed, since both compute the same homology over the same injective resolution and meet at
8656	  corepresentability.
8657	
8658	  \emph{(2) Serre vanishing for a general affine open.} The Serre vanishing of
8659	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is pinned to the \emph{top} open \(\top\) of a spectrum
8660	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\): it kills \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_\top, -)\) for \(q \geq 1\).
8661	  The cohomology that actually occurs here is over a \emph{general} affine open --- the preimage
8662	  \(j^{-1}(V)\) (respectively \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\)) of an affine open \(V\) of \(S\) that may straddle
8663	  the boundary of \(j(U)\), so \(j^{-1}(V)\) is a general, possibly non-distinguished, affine open of
8664	  \(U\). The residual is therefore \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_{j^{-1}(V)}, \mathcal{H}) = 0\)
8665	  over the abstract affine scheme \(U\), for \(q \geq 1\). We discharge it by splitting it into two
8666	  independent transports.
8667	
8668	  \emph{(2a) The residual lives over an affine scheme.} The immersion \(j\) is an affine morphism:
8669	  for \(U\) affine and \(X\) separated,
8670	  \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) is affine, so the preimage of every affine open of \(X\) is an affine
8671	  open of \(U\). In particular \(j^{-1}(V) = U \cap V\) (and likewise \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\)) is an
8672	  affine open of the affine scheme \(U\). After the reduction to the right-derived sections functor
8673	  --- by the presheaf description (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) and the
8674	  corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}, upgraded to right derived
8675	  functors by Lemma~\ref{lem:rightDerivedNatIso} --- the residual reads
8676	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_{U}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{j^{-1}(V)}, \mathcal{H}) = 0\), an \(\operatorname{Ext}\)
8677	  vanishing in the module category of the abstract affine scheme \(U\), for \(q \geq 1\).
8678	
8679	  \emph{(2b) Transport along the whole-scheme spectrum isomorphism (Need~\#1).} The abstract affine
8680	  scheme \(U\) carries the canonical \emph{whole-scheme} isomorphism
8681	  \(U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\) (\(\operatorname{Scheme.isoSpec}\),
8682	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib}), a genuine isomorphism of schemes because \(U\) is affine.
8683	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport} it induces an equivalence of module categories
8684	  \(U.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq (\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U).\mathrm{Modules}\) and transports
8685	  \(\operatorname{Ext}\) along it (via \(\operatorname{Ext.mapExactFunctor}\)), carrying the residual to
8686	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_{\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}, \mathcal{H}') = 0\), where
8687	  \(V'\) is the image affine open of \(j^{-1}(V)\) and \(\mathcal{H}'\) the transported (still
8688	  quasi-coherent) coefficient. Under the equivalence \(V'\) is in general a non-distinguished affine
8689	  open of \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\).
8690	
8691	  \emph{(2c) General-affine-open Serre vanishing (Need~\#2).} Over \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\) the
8692	  open \(V'\) is a general affine open, and Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open} gives
8693	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}, \mathcal{H}') = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\) and every affine
8694	  open \(V'\) and quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}'\). This is read off from the basis-comparison criterion
8695	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) with the cover system's basis \(\mathcal{B}\) enlarged
8696	  from the distinguished opens to \emph{all} affine opens; it requires no fresh cover system on the
8697	  open \(V'\) and stays entirely inside \((\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U).\mathrm{Modules}\). Combining
8698	  (2b) and (2c) kills the residual.
8699	
8700	  \textit{Remark (a blocked approach).} One might try to make \(j^{-1}(V)\) the \emph{whole} space by
8701	  transporting along the spectrum isomorphism of the \emph{open subscheme}
8702	  \(j^{-1}(V) \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{O})\). This is a dead end: the
8703	  inclusion \(j^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow U\) is an open immersion, not an isomorphism, so there is no
8704	  equivalence \(U.\mathrm{Modules} \simeq (j^{-1}(V)).\mathrm{Modules}\) to transport
8705	  \(\operatorname{Ext}\) along; using the open-subscheme isomorphism forces a restriction functor
8706	  \(\mathcal{H} \mapsto \mathcal{H}|_{j^{-1}(V)}\), whose derived comparison
8707	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q_{U}(j_!\mathcal{O}_{j^{-1}(V)}, \mathcal{H}) \cong H^q(j^{-1}(V),
8708	  \mathcal{H}|_{j^{-1}(V)})\) is exactly ``restriction preserves injectives'' --- infrastructure not
8709	  available in the present formalism. The sound route therefore keeps the spectrum isomorphism at the
8710	  level of the \emph{whole} affine \(U\) as in~(2b) and handles the general affine open ambiently
8711	  via~(2c).
8712	
8713	  \emph{(3) Locally zero implies zero sheafification.} The
8714	  passage from ``\(H^k(\cdots) = 0\) on a basis of affine opens'' to ``\(R^k f_* = 0\)'' is the
8715	  module-sheafification analogue of Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero} (and of its packaged
8716	  form Lemma~\ref{lem:isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_locally_isZero}). It is obtained by transporting the
8717	  abelian-sheaf statement across the natural isomorphism
8718	  \(\operatorname{toSheaf} \circ \operatorname{sheafify} \cong \operatorname{presheafToSheaf} \circ
8719	  \operatorname{forget}\) between module-sheafification followed by the forgetful functor and abelian
8720	  sheafification; the ``locally zero'' hypothesis it consumes is exactly the basis-of-affines vanishing
8721	  supplied by identifications (1) and~(2) above.
8722	\end{proof}

8742	  (additive). A composite of additive functors is additive; the additivity follows from the additivity of
8743	  each factor. The identification of its right derived functor with
8744	  absolute cohomology is the presheaf description of

9348	  for the induced equivalence of module categories, with \(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}}\) the corresponding
9349	  pushforward on presheaves of modules along the homeomorphism underlying \(\varphi\). Then for every
9350	  presheaf of modules \(P\) on \(X\) there is a natural isomorphism
9351	  \[

9361	  presheaves of abelian groups, and pushforward along the isomorphism \(\varphi\) is itself an
9362	  equivalence that commutes with the forgetful functors --- it relabels opens along the underlying
9363	  homeomorphism and leaves the underlying abelian-group data untouched. A left adjoint composed with an
9364	  equivalence is again a left adjoint, now to the conjugate forgetful functor; the mate of the identity
9365	  comparison between the two forgetful functors is the asserted natural isomorphism
9366	  \(\Phi(\operatorname{free} P) \cong \operatorname{free}(\Phi_{\mathrm{pre}} P)\), canonical by

7694	  fibre product of their structure maps: \((Y \times Z).\mathrm{left} \cong \operatorname{pullback}
7695	  Y.\mathrm{hom}\, Z.\mathrm{hom}\), compatibly with the projections.
7696	\end{lemma}
7697	
7698	\begin{lemma}
7699	
7700	\leanok
7701	[Nested coproduct flattens and reindexes to the next fibre power]
7702	  \label{lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex}
7703	  \lean{CategoryTheory.FinitaryPreExtensive.coproduct_fibrePower_reindex}
7704	  \uses{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}
7705	  Let \(\iota\) be a finite index type, \(S \in \mathcal{C}\), \((X_i \to S)_i\) a family over
7706	  \(S\), and for \(\tau : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota\) write \(Y_\tau = X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots
7707	  \times_S X_{\tau(p)}\). Then the nested coproduct flattens and reindexes to a coproduct over
7708	  \((p+2)\)-fold multi-indices:
7709	  \[
7710	    \coprod_{i}\coprod_{\tau} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)
7711	      \;\cong\;
7712	    \coprod_{(i,\tau)} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)
7713	      \;\cong\;
7714	    \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \to \iota}
7715	      \bigl(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\bigr),
7716	  \]
7717	  where the second isomorphism is the reindexing \((i, \tau) \mapsto \sigma =
7718	  \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau\), a bijection \(\iota \times (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota) \cong
7719	  (\mathrm{Fin}(p+2)\to\iota)\), under which \(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\) is exactly the \((p+2)\)-fold
7720	  fibre power \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\).
7721	\end{lemma}
7722	\begin{proof}
7723	  \uses{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}
7724	  The first isomorphism is \(\operatorname{sigmaSigmaIso}\) of
7725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:sigmaSigmaIso_mathlib}, collapsing the doubly-indexed coproduct
7726	  \(\coprod_i \coprod_\tau\) into a coproduct over pairs \((i,\tau)\). The
7727	  \(\operatorname{Fin.cons}\) bijection \(\iota \times (\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\to\iota) \cong
7728	  (\mathrm{Fin}(p+2)\to\iota)\), \((i,\tau)\mapsto \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau\), reindexes that
7729	  coproduct over \(\sigma\); and since \(\sigma(0) = i\) and \(\sigma(k+1) = \tau(k)\), the component
7730	  \(X_i \times_S Y_\tau = X_i \times_S (X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots)\) is term-for-term the
7731	  \((p+2)\)-fold fibre power \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\).
7732	\end{proof}
7733	
7734	\begin{lemma}
7735	[Coproduct distributes over the wide fibre power]
7736	  \label{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower}
7737	  \lean{CategoryTheory.FinitaryPreExtensive.widePullback_coproduct_iso,
7738	    CategoryTheory.FinitaryPreExtensive.prodFinSuccIso}
7739	  \uses{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod, lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower_zero,
7740	    lem:prod_coproduct_distrib, lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex,
7741	    lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib, lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}
7742	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category with pullbacks that is finitary pre-extensive
7743	  (\(\operatorname{FinitaryPreExtensive}\mathcal{C}\)), let \(\iota\) be a finite index type,
7744	  \(S \in \mathcal{C}\), and \((X_i \to S)_{i \in \iota}\) a finite family over \(S\). Then for every
7745	  \(p\) the \((p+1)\)-fold wide fibre power of the coproduct map \(\coprod_i X_i \to S\) over \(S\)
7746	  decomposes as a coproduct over multi-indices:
7747	  \[
7748	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_{i} X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+1)}
7749	      \;\cong\;
7750	    \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
7751	      \bigl(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p)}\bigr),
7752	  \]
7753	  the \(\sigma\)-component being the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of the legs \(X_{\sigma(k)} \to S\)
7754	  over \(S\), and the structure map of the coproduct being
7755	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\) of the component structure maps. The result is to be stated
7756	  abstractly for \(\operatorname{FinitaryPreExtensive}\mathcal{C}\) (reusable and Mathlib-aligned)
7757	  and then instantiated at \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\), which is finitary extensive by
7758	  Lemma~\ref{lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}.
7759	
7760	  \emph{Universe constraint.} This statement requires the index type \(\iota : \operatorname{Type} 0\):
7761	  the binary-distributivity primitive it rests on (Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_sigmaDesc_fst_mathlib}) does not
7762	  hold for index types in higher universes. When the cover index is finite but lives in a higher
7763	  universe, one first reindexes along an equivalence to \(\mathrm{Fin}(n)\) before applying this
7764	  result; see Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}.
7765	\end{lemma}
7766	\begin{proof}
7767	  \uses{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod, lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower_zero,
7768	    lem:prod_coproduct_distrib, lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex, lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib,
7769	    lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}
7770	  Induction on \(p\). Throughout, Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} lets us treat the
7771	  \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power over \(S\) as the \((p+1)\)-fold product in \(\operatorname{Over} S\),
7772	  so the induction has the clean recursion \(P^{\times_S (p+2)} \cong P \times_S P^{\times_S (p+1)}\)
7773	  with \(P = \coprod_i X_i\).
7774	
7775	  \emph{Base step} \(p = 0\) is Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower_zero}: the \(1\)-fold
7776	  fibre power is \(\coprod_i X_i\) itself, indexed by \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(1) \to \iota\).
7777	
7778	  \emph{Inductive step}: assume \(\bigl(\coprod_i X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+1)} \cong \coprod_{\tau :
7779	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota} Y_\tau\) with \(Y_\tau = X_{\tau(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S
7780	  X_{\tau(p)}\). By the recursion of Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_overX_eq_prod} and the inductive
7781	  hypothesis,
7782	  \[
7783	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_i X_i\bigr)^{\times_S (p+2)}
7784	      \;\cong\;
7785	    \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_i X_i\bigr) \times_S \bigl(\textstyle\coprod_\tau Y_\tau\bigr).
7786	  \]
7787	  Applying the one-sided distributivity of
7788	  Lemma~\ref{lem:prod_coproduct_distrib} on each side turns the right-hand side into
7789	  \(\coprod_{i}\coprod_{\tau} \bigl(X_i \times_S Y_\tau\bigr)\). Since the recursion lives in
7790	  \(\operatorname{Over} S\), this distributivity is consumed in its \(\operatorname{Over} S\)
7791	  binary-product form (Lemma~\ref{lem:overProd_coproduct_distrib}), which expresses
7792	  \((\coprod_\tau Y_\tau) \times B \cong \coprod_\tau (Y_\tau \times B)\) for the binary product in the
7793	  slice rather than the \(\mathcal{C}\)-level pullback. Finally
7794	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_fibrePower_reindex} flattens this nested coproduct
7795	  (via \(\operatorname{sigmaSigmaIso}\)) and reindexes the pairs \((i,\tau)\) as
7796	  \(\sigma = \operatorname{Fin.cons} i\,\tau : \mathrm{Fin}(p+2) \to \iota\), identifying \(X_i
7797	  \times_S Y_\tau\) with \(X_{\sigma(0)} \times_S \cdots \times_S X_{\sigma(p+1)}\). The structure
7798	  maps to \(S\) agree because each constituent isomorphism is built from \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\)
7799	  / product comparisons that commute with the projection to \(S\) by construction. Instantiating at
7800	  \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:finitaryExtensive_scheme_mathlib}) gives the
7801	  scheme-level statement.
7802	\end{proof}
7803	
7804	\begin{lemma}
7805	
7806	[The wide fibre power of open immersions is the intersection open]
7807	  \label{lem:widePullback_openImm_inter}
7808	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.widePullback_openImm_inter,
7809	    AlgebraicGeometry.mem_iInf_opens_of_finite}
7810	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:isPullback_opens_inf_mathlib}
7811	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an open cover and
7812	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I\) a multi-index. The \((p+1)\)-fold wide fibre power over
7813	  \(X\) of the open immersions \((U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X)_{k}\) is the intersection open:
7814	  \[
7815	    \operatorname{widePullback} X \;(\lambda\,k.\ U_{\sigma(k)})\;(\lambda\,k.\ (U_{\sigma(k)}).\iota)
7816	      \;\cong\;
7817	    (\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma).\mathrm{toScheme}
7818	      \;=\; \bigl(\textstyle\bigcap_k U_{\sigma(k)}\bigr).\mathrm{toScheme},
7819	  \]
7820	  over \(X\), and the structure map is the open immersion
7821	  \(j_\sigma : \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \hookrightarrow X\) of
7822	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}.
7823	\end{lemma}
7824	\begin{proof}
7825	  \uses{lem:isPullback_opens_inf_mathlib, def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7826	  Induction on the family, iterating the binary case. The pullback over \(X\) of two open
7827	  immersions \(U.\iota, V.\iota\) is the intersection open \((U \sqcap V).\mathrm{toScheme}\) with
7828	  the two \(\operatorname{homOfLE}\) inclusions --- this is
7829	  \(\operatorname{isPullback\_opens\_inf}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:isPullback_opens_inf_mathlib}. The
7830	  wide pullback of the family \((U_{\sigma(k)})_{k}\) is the iterated binary pullback over \(X\)
7831	  along \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1)\), so iterating the binary intersection gives
7832	  \(\bigcap_k U_{\sigma(k)}\), which is the indexed infimum
7833	  \(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma = \bigcap_k \operatorname{coverOpen}
7834	  \mathcal{U}\,(\sigma(k))\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} (recall
7835	  \(\operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i = (\mathcal{U}.f\,i).\operatorname{opensRange}\) is the
7836	  image open \(U_i\)). The structure map to \(X\) is the inclusion of the intersection, namely
7837	  \(j_\sigma\).
7838	\end{proof}
7839	
7840	\begin{lemma}
7841	
7842	\leanok
7843	[The degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech backbone is the coproduct of intersection opens]
7844	  \label{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}
7845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechBackbone_left_sigma}
7846	  \uses{lem:cechBackbone_obj_widePullback, lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower,
7847	    lem:widePullback_openImm_inter, def:cover_cech_nerve, def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7848	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an open cover with cover arrow
7849	  \(q : \coprod_{i \in I} U_i \to X\), and let \(Y_p\) denote the degree-\(p\) object of the
7850	  {\v C}ech nerve \(\operatorname{coverCechNerveOver}\mathcal{U}\) of
7851	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} --- the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of \(q\) over
7852	  \(X\). Then \(Y_p\) is canonically the coproduct
7853	  \[
7854	    Y_p \;\cong\; \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I} U_\sigma,
7855	    \qquad
7856	    U_\sigma \;=\; \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma \;=\;
7857	      \bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)},
7858	  \]
7859	  indexed by the multi-indices \(\sigma\), and the structure map \(q_p : Y_p \to X\) is
7860	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\), where
7861	  \(j_\sigma : U_\sigma \hookrightarrow X\) is the open immersion of the intersection open
7862	  \(U_\sigma\) of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}. In words: the fibre power
7863	  of the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\) is the intersection open, and the disjoint
7864	  union over \(\sigma\) assembles the {\v C}ech backbone.
7865	\end{lemma}
7866	\begin{proof}
7867	  \uses{lem:cechBackbone_obj_widePullback, lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower,
7868	    lem:widePullback_openImm_inter}
7869	  Assemble the chain in \(\operatorname{Over} X\). By
7870	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cechBackbone_obj_widePullback} the degree-\(p\) object \(Y_p\) is the wide
7871	  pullback \(\bigl(\coprod_i U_i\bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}\), the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power of
7872	  \(q\) over \(X\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} (instantiated at
7873	  \(\mathcal{C} = \operatorname{Scheme}\), with \(S = X\) and the family
7874	  \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)) this fibre power is the coproduct
7875	  \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I} \bigl(U_{\sigma(0)} \times_X \cdots \times_X
7876	  U_{\sigma(p)}\bigr)\) over the multi-indices. By
7877	  Lemma~\ref{lem:widePullback_openImm_inter} each \(\sigma\)-component, being the wide fibre
7878	  power over \(X\) of the open immersions \(U_{\sigma(k)} \hookrightarrow X\), is the
7879	  intersection open \(U_\sigma = \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\sigma = \bigcap_k
7880	  U_{\sigma(k)}\), with structure map the open immersion \(j_\sigma\). Composing these three
7881	  isomorphisms gives \(Y_p \cong \coprod_\sigma U_\sigma\); the structure-map agreement is
7882	  automatic, since each step's isomorphism commutes with the projection to \(X\), so the
7883	  universal map out of the coproduct is \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\).
7884	
7885	  \emph{Universe reduction.} The distributivity Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower} and the
7886	  binary-distributivity leaf it rests on (Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_sigmaDesc_fst_mathlib}) require the
7887	  index type to lie in \(\operatorname{Type} 0\); the cover index \(I\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) is a
7888	  finite type that need not live in \(\operatorname{Type} 0\). The reduction is therefore performed
7889	  here: choose an equivalence \(I \cong \mathrm{Fin}(n)\) (from finiteness of \(I\)), reindex
7890	  the cover family \((U_i \hookrightarrow X)\) and every coproduct- and wide-pullback-index along it to
7891	  land at \(\mathrm{Fin}(n) : \operatorname{Type} 0\), apply the \(\operatorname{Type} 0\) decomposition
7892	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower}, and transport the resulting isomorphism back along
7893	  the same equivalence. Reindexing a coproduct (resp.\ a wide fibre power) along a bijection of index
7894	  types is an isomorphism compatible with the structure maps, so the transported isomorphism is again
7895	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(\sigma \mapsto j_\sigma)\) over \(X\).
7896	\end{proof}

7898	\begin{lemma}

7900	[The cover arrow is the coproduct of its member arrows in the slice]
7901	  \label{lem:coverArrow_over_sigma}

7903	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrowOverCofan,

7905	    CategoryTheory.overSigmaDescCofan,

7911	  category \(\operatorname{Over} X\) the object \(\operatorname{Over.mk}(q)\) carrying the cover arrow is
7912	  the coproduct of the member arrows \(\operatorname{Over.mk}(\mathcal{U}.f\,i)\):
7913	  \[
7914	    \operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\mathcal{U}.f\bigr)
7915	      \;\cong\;
7916	    \coprod_{i \in I} \operatorname{Over.mk}(\mathcal{U}.f\,i)
7917	      \qquad\text{in } \operatorname{Over} X.

7920	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_backbone_left_sigma}): the source of the cover arrow is the disjoint union of the

7930	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}\mathcal{U}.f \circ \iota_i = \mathcal{U}.f\,i\), the triangle
7931	  \(\operatorname{Over.w}\)). It is a colimit (\(\operatorname{coverArrowOverIsColimit}\)) by

7934	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.hom\_ext}\), both lifted to the slice via \(\operatorname{Over.homMk}\) since the

/-- Right-handed one-sided distributivity in `Over S`: `A ⨯ (∐ᵢ Yᵢ) ≅ ∐ᵢ (A ⨯ Yᵢ)`. -/
noncomputable def overProd_coproduct_distrib_right {C : Type*} [Category C] [HasPullbacks C]
    [FinitaryPreExtensive C] {ι : Type} [Finite ι] [HasFiniteCoproducts C]
    {S : C} [HasBinaryProducts (Over S)] (A : Over S) (Y : ι → Over S) :
    A ⨯ (∐ Y) ≅ ∐ fun i => A ⨯ Y i :=
  Limits.prod.braiding A (∐ Y) ≪≫ overProd_coproduct_distrib Y A ≪≫
    Limits.Sigma.mapIso (fun i => Limits.prod.braiding (Y i) A)

/-- Coproduct distributes over the `(p+1)`-fold wide fibre power of the cover map `∐ᵢ Zᵢ ⟶ S`,
in slice-product normal form (blueprint `lem:coproduct_distrib_fibrePower`). -/
noncomputable def widePullback_coproduct_iso {C : Type*} [Category C] [HasPullbacks C]
    [FinitaryPreExtensive C] {S : C} {ι : Type} [Finite ι] {Z : ι → C}
    (f : (i : ι) → Z i ⟶ S) [HasFiniteWidePullbacks C] [HasFiniteCoproducts C]
    [HasFiniteProducts (Over S)] [HasFiniteCoproducts (Over S)] :
    (p : ℕ) → (Over.mk (WidePullback.base (fun _ : Fin (p + 1) => Limits.Sigma.desc f))
      ≅ ∐ (fun σ : Fin (p + 1) → ι => ∏ᶜ (fun k : Fin (p + 1) => Over.mk (f (σ k)))))
  | 0 => widePullback_coproduct_iso_zero f
  | (p+1) => by
      refine widePullback_overX_eq_prod (fun _ : Fin (p + 2) => Limits.Sigma.desc f) ≪≫
        prodFinSuccIso (fun _ : Fin (p + 2) => Over.mk (Limits.Sigma.desc f)) ≪≫ ?_
      refine Limits.prod.mapIso (overSigmaDescIso f).symm
        ((widePullback_overX_eq_prod (fun _ : Fin (p + 1) => Limits.Sigma.desc f)).symm ≪≫
          widePullback_coproduct_iso f p) ≪≫ ?_
      refine overProd_coproduct_distrib (fun i => Over.mk (f i))
        (∐ fun τ : Fin (p + 1) → ι => ∏ᶜ fun k => Over.mk (f (τ k))) ≪≫ ?_
      refine Limits.Sigma.mapIso (fun i => overProd_coproduct_distrib_right (Over.mk (f i))
        (fun τ : Fin (p + 1) → ι => ∏ᶜ fun k => Over.mk (f (τ k)))) ≪≫ ?_
      have e7 := Limits.Sigma.mapIso (fun i => Limits.Sigma.mapIso (fun τ : Fin (p + 1) → ι =>
        (prodFinSuccIso (fun k : Fin (p + 2) => Over.mk (f (Fin.cons i τ k)))).symm))
      exact e7 ≪≫ coproduct_fibrePower_reindex p (fun σ => ∏ᶜ fun k => Over.mk (f (σ k)))

\begin{lemma}
[Serre Ext-vanishing over an affine scheme via the spectrum transport]
  \label{lem:ext_jShriekOU_eq_zero_of_specIso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_jShriekOU_eq_zero_of_specIso}
  \uses{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open,
    lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions, lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}
  Let \(U\) be a scheme with chosen injective resolutions on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\), let
  \(\varphi : U \cong \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an isomorphism of schemes,
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi\) the induced equivalence of module categories,
  \(V \subseteq U\) an open and \(\mathcal{H}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Suppose
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\) for some \emph{affine} open
  \(V' \subseteq \operatorname{Spec} R\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}) and that
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H})\) is quasi-coherent
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}). Then for every \(q \geq 1\) every class
  \(e \in \operatorname{Ext}^q_U(j_!\mathcal{O}_V, \mathcal{H})\) vanishes.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open,
    lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions, lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}
  The chosen injective resolutions provide enough injectives on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:enoughInjectives_of_hasInjectiveResolutions}), which transport across the equivalence
  \(\Phi\) to enough injectives on \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\). Over
  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) the general-affine-open Serre vanishing
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing_general_open}), applied to the affine open \(V'\) and the
  quasi-coherent coefficient \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H})\), makes
  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'},\, \Phi.\mathrm{functor}\,\mathcal{H})\) a subsingleton.
  Transferring along the transport isomorphism
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\) in the first Ext-argument
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:subsingleton_ext_of_iso_fst}) shows
  \(\operatorname{Ext}^q(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V),\, \Phi.\mathrm{functor}\,\mathcal{H})\)
  is a subsingleton as well. The spectrum Ext-transport additive isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{Ext}^q_U(j_!\mathcal{O}_V, \mathcal{H})
      \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{Ext}^q_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V),\,
      \Phi.\mathrm{functor}\,\mathcal{H}\bigr)
  \]
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport}) is injective, so it carries \(e\) into a subsingleton
  and forces \(e = 0\).
\end{proof}

Let
$(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
$(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
be affine schemes.
Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
$\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
\begin{enumerate}
\item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
\item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
in the $S$-module $N$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The first assertion follows from the identification in
Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}.
The second assertion follows from the fact
that $\psi^{-1}(D(f)) = D(\psi^\sharp(f))$ and hence
$$
\psi_* \widetilde N(D(f)) = \widetilde N(D(\psi^\sharp(f))) =
N_{\psi^\sharp(f)} = (N_R)_f = \widetilde{N_R}(D(f))
$$
as desired.
\end{proof}

\section{Functoriality for quasi-coherent modules}
\label{section-quasi-coherent}

\noindent
Let $X$ be a scheme. We denote $\QCoh(\mathcal{O}_X)$ the
category of quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules as defined in
Modules, Definition \ref{modules-definition-quasi-coherent}.
We have seen in
Section \ref{section-quasi-coherent-affine}
that the category $\QCoh(\mathcal{O}_X)$ has a lot of good properties
when $X$ is affine. Since the property of being quasi-coherent is
local on $X$, these properties are inherited by the category
of quasi-coherent sheaves on any scheme $X$. We enumerate them here.
\begin{enumerate}
\item A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is
quasi-coherent if and only if the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$
to each affine open $U = \Spec(R)$ is of the form
$\widetilde M$ for some $R$-module $M$.
\item A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is
quasi-coherent if and only if the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$
to each of the members of an affine open covering is quasi-coherent.
\item Any direct sum of quasi-coherent sheaves is quasi-coherent.
\item Any colimit of quasi-coherent sheaves is quasi-coherent.
\item
\label{item-kernel}
The kernel and cokernel of a morphism of quasi-coherent sheaves
is quasi-coherent.
\item Given a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
$0 \to \mathcal{F}_1 \to \mathcal{F}_2 \to \mathcal{F}_3 \to 0$
if two out of three are quasi-coherent so is the third.
\item Given a morphism of schemes $f : Y \to X$ the pullback
of a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is a quasi-coherent
$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module. See
Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}.
\item Given two quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
the tensor product is quasi-coherent, see
Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-tensor-product-permanence}.
\item Given a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
the tensor, symmetric and exterior algebras on $\mathcal{F}$
are quasi-coherent, see
Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-whole-tensor-algebra-permanence}.
\item Given two quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
$\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ such that $\mathcal{F}$
is of finite presentation, then the internal hom
$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$
is quasi-coherent, see
Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-internal-hom-locally-kernel-direct-sum}
and (\ref{item-kernel}) above.
\end{enumerate}
On the other hand, it is in general not the case that the
pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.
Here is a case where this does hold.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}

\begin{lemma}[Pushforward along an iso preserves quasi-coherence]
  \label{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Section ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent
  % modules'', items (1),(7) and the closing remark on pushforward (read from
  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L4729--4767).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ is
  % quasi-coherent if and only if the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$
  % to each affine open $U = \Spec(R)$ is of the form
  % $\widetilde M$ for some $R$-module $M$."
  % "Given a morphism of schemes $f : Y \to X$ the pullback
  % of a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is a quasi-coherent
  % $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module. See
  % Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}."
  % "On the other hand, it is in general not the case that the
  % pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.
  % Here is a case where this does hold."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, ``Functoriality for quasi-coherent modules''.}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(H : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a
  quasi-coherent module. Then the pushforward \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) along
  \(\varphi\) is again quasi-coherent.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib}
  Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} property of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, checkable on any affine
  open cover: \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent iff its restriction to each affine open
  \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R\) is of the form \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(R\)-module \(M\) ---
  equivalently iff each member of an affine open covering carries a local presentation
  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}|_U \to 0\). This is the datum
  \(H\) carries as a quasi-coherent module (its \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\): an affine cover
  together with, on each member, the local presentation).

  The morphism \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of ringed spaces: its underlying map is a homeomorphism
  \(X \cong Y\) inducing an order-isomorphism of the open frames \(\operatorname{Opens} X \cong
  \operatorname{Opens} Y\), and it carries the structure-ring sheaf along, an isomorphism
  \(X.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf} \cong (\varphi)_*\, Y.\operatorname{ringCatSheaf}\). Pushforward
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor} = \varphi_*\) along \(\varphi\) is therefore the \emph{transport} of module
  data across this isomorphism of ringed spaces: it relabels opens by the homeomorphism and twists the
  \(\mathcal{O}\)-action by the ring-sheaf isomorphism, with no further modification of the underlying
  abelian-sheaf data.

  Transport \(H\)'s quasi-coherent datum across \(\varphi\). Under the homeomorphism the affine open
  cover of \(X\) supporting \(H\) goes to an affine open cover of \(Y\) (the image of an affine open
  under a scheme isomorphism is affine, Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}). On each
  image member \(W = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,U\) the local presentation
  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to H|_U \to 0\) is carried by \(\varphi_*\) to a
  presentation \(\mathcal{O}_Y^{(J)} \to \mathcal{O}_Y^{(I)} \to (\varphi_* H)|_W \to 0\), because the
  ring-sheaf isomorphism sends finite free \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to finite free
  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules and transport, being an equivalence, is exact and so preserves the
  cokernel presentation. Hence \(\varphi_* H\) has a local presentation on an affine cover of \(Y\) and
  is quasi-coherent.

  Equivalently, since \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is the
  \emph{pullback} along the inverse isomorphism, and pullback of a quasi-coherent module is
  quasi-coherent (item (7) of the cited functoriality list, Modules
  Lemma~\texttt{modules-lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}); the by-hand presentation transport above is
  the unwinding of that fact in the present \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\) formalism.

  \emph{Why the transport is done by hand.} Mathlib's \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.pushforward}\)
  carries no general quasi-coherence-preservation lemma: a pushforward \(f_*\) preserves quasi-coherence
  only under extra hypotheses on \(f\) --- it is quasi-compact-quasi-separated, or an isomorphism. ``It
  is in general not the case that the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent.'' The
  present statement is exactly the isomorphism case, which is not packaged as a reusable Mathlib lemma
  here; so the proof transports the local \(\operatorname{QuasicoherentData}\) presentation across the
  isomorphism of ringed spaces explicitly, as above, rather than invoking a ready-made instance.
\end{proof}
